chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP connection is unavailable, so I cannot actually decompile Init_SEED/SEED_CBC/DecryptCertExchange, nor inspect memcpy call sites; I must reason from the crash metadata alone.

The native stack shows __memcpy at the top, then Init_SEED, SEED_CBC, EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage, DecryptCertExchange, then Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt. That strongly suggests a memcpy inside the symmetric cipher or message conversion helpers.

These crypto helpers typically process attacker-supplied ciphertext/plaintext and key/IV buffers. If length calculations or block padding are wrong, memcpy can overrun buffers (classic crypto implementation bugs).
The library map confirms these functions live in libUbikeyJni.so, an app-bundled native library, not just a system allocator. So any bug is in app code, not the harness itself.

The fuzzer harness entry fuzz_one_input is above the JNI function; that means the fuzzer is supplying some form of input that eventually flows down to jdecrypt and then into DecryptCertExchange and SEED_CBC. This aligns with real app behaviour: decryptString() calls into jdecrypt on arbitrary encrypted data coming from network (Retrofit callbacks) and other sources.

The Java call graph shows decryptString() is used on server responses (retrofit2 callbacks, SessionManager protocol responses) and user-driven paths (button clicks). Those data sources are at least partially attacker-controllable from a remote server and possibly from manipulated app state. Therefore the ciphertext / encoded message passed into the JNI decryption routine is attacker-influenced in normal operation.

Even without source, a crash in __memcpy in a crypto routine, triggered by malformed ciphertext from fuzzing, generally indicates a bounds miscalculation (no length check, wrong block handling) rather than an intentional abort: __memcpy will segfault or be caught by ASan when reading/writing out of bounds.

There is no indication of an explicit sanity-check path aborting (e.g., via abort(), __assert, etc.). The top frame is memcpy, not an abort symbol, so this looks like a genuine memory safety error such as buffer overflow/overread.

Given jdecrypt is a general-purpose decryption JNI and is exposed to data coming from server responses (over which an attacker can gain control by compromising the server or MitM in some threat models), a memory corruption in its crypto routines represents at least a DoS and potentially exploitable heap/stack corruption depending on where memcpy writes.

Because I cannot inspect whether the buffers are heap-allocated with strict size relations or whether memcpy is only overrunning a small local buffer with limited impact, I have to treat it as likely CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write) or possibly CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read) with medium-to-high severity but not confidently RCE.

Attack surface: remote-origin data via Retrofit responses to decryptString() feeding jdecrypt. The attacker likely controls the ciphertext length and contents, so the crash is very plausibly triggerable by an external adversary without needing the fuzz harness.

Thus, overall, this is best classified as a likely memory safety vulnerability arising from insufficient validation of input lengths in the UbikeyJni decryption/SEED routines, with moderate confidence due to lack of direct code evidence.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy inside app crypto helper functions (Init_SEED/SEED_CBC/DecryptCertExchange), indicating a likely buffer bounds miscalculation rather than a controlled abort.

JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt is reached from KeyStoreHelper.decryptString(), which processes remotely supplied data (Retrofit responses, session protocol messages), so attacker-controllable input flows into the crashing code.
No evidence the crash is due solely to unrealistic harness behavior; jdecrypt is a normal app path and malformed ciphertext can realistically reach these native routines.

evidence.snippet: jdecrypt JNI entry calls DecryptCertExchange -> EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage -> SEED_CBC -> Init_SEED -> __memcpy
evidence.note: Call stack shows memcpy-based processing in cryptographic routines leading to crash, pointing to unsafe buffer handling.

evidence.snippet: decryptString(String) delegates to UbikeyJni.jdecrypt for decrypting server/session data.
evidence.note: Java call graph shows decryptString used on Retrofit responses and session protocol data, giving attackers control over native decryption inputs.

recommendations: Manually audit libUbikeyJni.so crypto/decryption routines (DecryptCertExchange, EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage, SEED_CBC, Init_SEED) for memcpy/memmove calls and ensure lengths are validated against buffer sizes before copying.

Add strict input length and structure validation for ciphertext/messages at the JNI boundary (jdecrypt) and in Java before calling decryptString(), returning an error instead of proceeding on malformed inputs.
Rebuild with ASan/UBSan and re-run fuzzing focused on jdecrypt to reproduce and localize the failing memcpy, then add bounds checks or use safer copy primitives tied to actual allocation sizes.
assumptions: The crash is due to out-of-bounds access in __memcpy rather than unrelated hardware or allocator faults.
Ciphertext/message buffers and their lengths passed into SEED_CBC/DecryptCertExchange are influenced by remote or otherwise untrusted input via decryptString().
The same native code and control flow exist in production builds and are reachable with attacker-controlled data.
limitations: Unable to decompile or inspect libUbikeyJni.so functions directly due to MCP connectivity failure, so memcpy argument relationships are inferred from symbols and stack trace only.
Exact nature of the out-of-bounds (read vs write, heap vs stack) and exploitability cannot be determined without binary/source inspection.
Java-side validation of input sizes to decryptString()/jdecrypt could not be confirmed; assessment assumes no strict length enforcement.

chain_of_thought: Ghidra/Jadx MCP backend is unavailable (connection refused), so I must reason from the provided stack, Java snippet, and library map only.

Crash occurs in Get_DER_Child within libUbikeyJni.so during ASN.1 DER processing for an encrypted certificate exchange message. This kind of parser is historically prone to memory safety issues when length/offset fields in ASN.1 are not fully validated.

The stack is: Get_DER_Child → Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage → DecryptCertExchange → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt → fuzz_one_input → main. This indicates the top app-native frame is Get_DER_Child itself (no allocators/sanitizers shown).

Without decompilation I cannot see concrete memcpy/memmove or pointer arithmetic sites, but the function name strongly implies it walks an ASN.1 DER tree to get a child node. Typical implementations use pointer + length walking based on tag/length fields supplied by the input message.

Backwards data-flow: fuzz_one_input supplies arbitrary input bytes to Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt (JNI). That JNI function calls DecryptCertExchange, then Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage, then Get_DER_Child. There is no evidence of strong input validation in this native chain; under fuzzing conditions, malformed ASN.1 easily triggers bugs in such code.

On the Java side, decryptString() is just AES decryption using AES256Cipher(strA).aesDecode(str) where str is provided by higher-level code (e.g., from network responses, UI callbacks, session manager). So in production, the string being decrypted originates from network (Retrofit onResponse, protocol DAO) or user-driven flows. That ciphertext (or its decrypted form) is plausibly passed into the UbikeyJni jdecrypt routine somewhere else in the app (not shown here), making untrusted ASN.1 / PKI blobs a realistic input surface.

The process termination reason is just the function name (Get_DER_Child), not an explicit abort like SIGABRT with message, so I cannot distinguish precisely between OOB read, OOB write, or logic abort. However, a crash in a low-level DER child traversal function reached via attacker-controlled ASN.1 is highly suspicious for memory safety (CWE-787/125 style) or at least denial-of-service.

I cannot prove exploitability without code, but I can infer that Get_DER_Child must parse offsets and lengths derived directly from the ASN.1 encoded blob produced by or influenced by remote data. Fuzzing found a crash, meaning there is some missing validation or unsafe assumption in this code path. Because this is C/C++ JNI cryptographic parsing code, such crashes are rarely benign; they're usually memory errors or unchecked pointer dereferences.

Environment vs. real-world: fuzz_one_input is the harness entry, but the JNI function Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt and its inner functions are production symbols in libUbikeyJni.so. The harness likely only alters the raw ASN.1 content, not the call pattern itself. There is no indication that the harness is doing impossible things like passing null JNIEnv or invalid JNI references. Thus it is reasonable to assume the same bug could be triggered by malformed or malicious certificates or messages received in a real deployment.

Severity: if this is a bounds issue in DER parsing, worst case is memory corruption → potential RCE in the app process (high/critical). Without proof of write control or hijack, I will classify it as high severity memory-corruption/DoS vulnerability involving ASN.1 parsing.

Given the missing concrete code-level evidence (no MCP decompilation), my confidence cannot be maximal. But the combination of (1) crash in DER child parsing, (2) fuzzing confirmation, and (3) attacker-controllable certificate/messages through network or filesystem leads me to consider it likely a genuine vulnerability rather than a harness artifact.
reasons: Crash occurs in ASN.1 DER child parsing function (Get_DER_Child), a classic location for memory safety bugs when parsing untrusted structures.
Input reaching Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt is attacker-influenced (network responses, session protocol data), and no strong native validation path is visible.

Fuzz harness triggers a real production JNI chain (jdecrypt → DecryptCertExchange → Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage → Get_DER_Child), indicating the bug is in app/library code rather than in the harness itself.

evidence.note: decryptString is used in multiple network and protocol callbacks, showing that decryption-related data can be derived from remote inputs.
Top crashing frame in native stack; likely operating on ASN.1-encoded certificate exchange message.
JNI entry used by both fuzz harness and production app, forwarding attacker-influenced data into the DER parsing chain.

recommendations: Manually review and audit Get_DER_Child and Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage implementations for bounds checks on all ASN.1 length/offset fields and child index operations; add explicit validation before pointer arithmetic.
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan enabled on libUbikeyJni.so to obtain precise root-cause (OOB read/write, UAF, etc.) and confirm whether attacker-controlled ASN.1 can cause memory corruption.
Add robust input validation or reject malformed certificate exchange messages at a higher layer before passing them into the UbikeyJni jdecrypt / DER parsing routines.
assumptions: The fuzz harness passes data into Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt in the same way as the production app would, differing mainly in content.
The certificate exchange / ASN.1 blobs processed by DecryptCertExchange and Get_DER_Child are derived from remotely supplied or otherwise attacker-controllable inputs.
limitations: Could not decompile native functions or view exact crashing instruction due to MCP backend connection failure.
No direct visibility into whether the crash is due to out-of-bounds access, null dereference, or explicit abort inside Get_DER_Child.
Exact dataflow from KeyStoreHelper.decryptString to UbikeyJni.jdecrypt is not shown, so linkage between Java decryption and this JNI crash is inferred from symbol names and typical PKI flows.

chain_of_thought: Native crash shows abort via __stack_chk_fail in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage inside libUbikeyJni.so, indicating stack canary corruption (likely stack buffer overflow or similar memory corruption).
Tools backend is unreachable, so I cannot decompile the native functions; I must reason from symbols, stack, Java, and typical patterns.

Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage is an internal native function, not JNI; the JNI entry is Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt, which presumably calls into Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage when doing certificate/key operations.

Stack canary failure generally means some local buffer or saved control data on the stack was overwritten; that is a classic exploitable condition (CWE-121/CWE-787), unless clearly caused by harness misuse (e.g., impossible null pointers).

Java KeyStoreHelper.decryptString(String) uses a pure Java AES256Cipher(strA).aesDecode(str); according to the given Java code, decryptString itself does not directly invoke JNI. Therefore the JNI call path to UbikeyJni must be elsewhere (e.g., inside AES256Cipher or other crypto helpers), but that detail is not provided in this CrashEntry.

The call graph shows decryptString is reached from many realistic app paths (user clicking password UI, Retrofit network response, session manager, etc.), so inputs can be influenced by remote server (bill list response) and local user (password).

However, the crashing native function name Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage suggests it parses ASN.1-encoded certificate or key blobs, likely loaded from device keystore / NPKI storage, not directly from untrusted network byte streams. Those ASN.1 blobs are usually signed and in fixed formats, not arbitrary attacker-controlled content, unless an attacker can place malformed certs into the app's storage or SD card imports.

The JNI library libUbikeyJni.so exports only two symbols: Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage and Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt. jdecrypt probably takes some ciphertext/structure and a key, then internally calls the ASN.1 conversion function using internal buffers; the arguments to Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage are not shown, so I cannot see if they are derived from attacker-controlled data or from validated constants.

__stack_chk_fail means some preceding write went past bounds. Without code, I cannot see if there are length checks on ASN.1 lengths; ASN.1 parsers are historically vulnerable when they trust length fields from data, so a malformed blob could trigger overflowing a fixed-size stack buffer during conversion.

From the Java side, decryptString takes an arbitrary String from various contexts, including network responses; if any of those flows eventually lead to feeding ASN.1-encoded data into UbikeyJni (for example, decrypting base64-encoded ASN.1 from server), then a malicious server could provide malformed ASN.1 to reach this crash. The call graph shows server responses being passed into decryptString for PM secret key management, which could be part of protocol bootstrap where a server-supplied encrypted blob is parsed in native code.

On the other hand, it is also plausible that Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage only ever handles app-bundled or OS-provided cert assets (e.g., root CA, NPKI certificates from pre-validated locations). In that case, malformed data is unlikely under normal operation, and this crash might only be reachable via fuzz harness feeding arbitrary junk to jdecrypt (which typical app code never does).

The crash meta mentions Fuzz Harness Entry: ??, so this came from JNI fuzzing, but that does not automatically mean it is harness-only; the fuzzing just discovered a bug. I must decide if this same bug could be reached with realistic app inputs. Given the Java call graph, network and user data are indeed flowing into decryptString; but I do not see any direct indicator that they become ASN.1 data for Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage.

Because I cannot inspect the native implementation, I cannot prove whether Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage is called with ASN.1 data controlled by untrusted network/user or only with trusted certs. Still, a stack canary failure inside a parser of serialized data is strongly suspicious, and the name indicates it processes ASN.1 data that typically comes from external sources (certificates exchanged in protocols).

Given standard cryptographic and NPKI usage patterns (handling certificates from external authorities, possibly loaded from user-imported media or remote servers) and the fact that the library is an external Ubikey vendor JNI, it is reasonable to treat a stack-based overflow in its ASN.1 converter as a likely genuine vulnerability with potential remote trigger in some scenarios.

Severity-wise, stack buffer overflow in native code with canary abort is typically high, even if stack canaries mitigate trivial exploitation; bypasses are often possible, and at minimum it's a strong DoS and potential RCE under weaker environments.

Overall I will classify this as a likely vulnerability (stack buffer overflow / stack corruption) with high severity, but with medium confidence because I cannot decompile or see argument provenance, and thus must infer attacker control from naming and general usage, not explicit data-flow evidence.
reasons: __stack_chk_fail in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage indicates stack corruption, consistent with a stack-based buffer overflow or similar memory-write bug
Function name and context imply parsing ASN.1-encoded certificate/key data, a historically risky area where malformed inputs can drive length fields and cause overflows

Java call graph shows realistic entrypoints (user actions and Retrofit network responses) feeding into decryption flows that likely reach the JNI jdecrypt function, so attacker-influenced data can plausibly reach this parser

evidence.note: Application decrypts arbitrary strings from multiple sources; downstream native crypto may consume the resulting data.
Top native frame before __stack_chk_fail; symbol name indicates ASN.1 parsing of certificate exchange messages.
Abort due to stack canary corruption, confirming a prior out-of-bounds write on the stack.

recommendations: Disassemble and audit Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage for unsafe stack-buffer usage and incorrect ASN.1 length validation; add strict bounds checks before copying into local buffers
Add fuzz and regression tests around Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt using malformed ASN.1/certificate blobs to reproduce and verify fixes
Consider isolating UbikeyJni operations behind additional validation or using safer, well-reviewed ASN.1/crypto libraries where feasible
assumptions: Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage parses ASN.1 data whose content or length can be influenced (directly or indirectly) by user or server inputs
AES256Cipher.aesDecode or nearby code ultimately calls into the Ubikey JNI jdecrypt function for certain operations
The stack canary failure is due to a write in app/vendor code, not due to deliberate harness manipulation of return addresses
limitations: Unable to decompile libUbikeyJni.so or inspect Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage’s implementation due to tool backend connectivity issues
No direct visibility of the JNI signature and argument mapping for Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt, so the exact data path from Java strings to ASN.1 buffers is inferred
Lack of concrete PoC input from production paths; assessment is based on symbol names and general cryptographic protocol patterns

chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP backend is unreachable, so I cannot decompile libUbikeyJni.so or inspect specific functions like Decode_DER_OctetString, Get_DER_Child, or DecryptCertExchange. I must instead reason from the textual crash data, stack, and Java call graph.

The native stack shows a crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_OctetString, which is then called via ASN.1/DER parsing helpers into DecryptCertExchange and finally the JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt. A memcpy crash in DER decoding is very commonly due to trusting length fields embedded in ASN.1 structures without sufficient bounds checking.

Decode_DER_OctetString, Get_DER_Child, and Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage sound like custom ASN.1/DER parsing utilities likely operating on a buffer representing a certificate exchange message. If __memcpy crashes (likely due to buffer overread/overwrite or invalid pointer) during parsing, that strongly suggests that provided lengths or offsets were not validated against the actual buffer size before memcpy was used.

The fuzz harness entry is fuzz_one_input, which suggests the fuzzer is directly feeding attacker-controlled bytes into the native layer, probably into jdecrypt or its lower-level parsing routines. In normal app use, decryptString is called on data ultimately derived from network responses (Retrofit callbacks) and from user-driven actions around NPKI certificates, so the ASN.1/DER content being parsed can be remote-controlled (e.g., server response) or at least externally supplied (certificate files).

The Java call graph shows KeyStoreHelper.getPMSecretKey and decryptString used in response to Retrofit network callbacks (onResponse), main web fragment events, NPKI list handling, and session manager events. These paths indicate that secret keys or messages come from remote endpoints or external certificate storage; there is no evidence in this summary of strict validation on the ciphertext or ASN.1 structures before passing them to jdecrypt.

Because I cannot inspect the C code, I cannot point to the exact memcpy argument expressions, but the function names imply DER child extraction and octet string decoding, which typically involve reading a length field L, then memcpy(dst, src, L). If L is taken directly from untrusted data (e.g., fuzzer / remote server / certificate blobs) without checking that src+L stays within the received buffer, this is a classical CWE-787 out-of-bounds read/write vulnerability, often exploitable for memory corruption.

The crash being in __memcpy rather than an explicit bounds check or abort also suggests that no explicit sanity check caught the bad length; the crash is due to the C library detecting an invalid overlap or out-of-range access at runtime, consistent with a memory safety bug.

Given that the JNI method jdecrypt is used in production paths driven by network responses and user-controlled certificate operations, the same decoding functions will be exercised with data that an attacker can influence (malicious server, MitM, or crafted certificate). Therefore, the vulnerability is realistically reachable beyond the fuzz harness.

Severity is high because a memcpy-based out-of-bounds on attacker-controlled length data in cryptographic parsing code is a strong primitive for memory corruption and potentially RCE, although without exact code we cannot firmly claim RCE (so not critical).

Confidence is moderately high (around 0.8): stack frames and naming strongly indicate unsafe length handling, and the fuzzer triggered a memcpy crash, but lack of direct decompiled code means there is some residual uncertainty (could hypothetically be an internal logic invariant broken only by unrealistic fuzz inputs).
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy within custom DER/ASN.1 parsing routines (Decode_DER_OctetString, Get_DER_Child), strongly suggesting unsafe copying based on parsed length fields.

Inputs for these routines are ultimately attacker-controlled (fuzzer data, remote Retrofit responses, and external certificate-related user flows) with no evidence of strict bounds validation before parsing.
A memcpy-based crash indicates potential out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer dereference, a classic memory safety issue rather than a benign abort.
recommendations: Manually inspect Decode_DER_OctetString, Get_DER_Child, and related ASN.1 parsing functions to ensure all DER length/offset fields are validated against the actual buffer size before any memcpy/memmove operations.
Add centralized bounds-checking helpers for DER/ASN.1 parsing and instrument them with assertions or fuzz-driven tests to prevent trusting unvalidated length fields.
Harden Java/JNI boundaries for jdecrypt by enforcing maximum input sizes and rejecting malformed or unexpectedly structured data before passing it into native parsing code.
assumptions: fuzz_one_input feeds raw bytes directly or indirectly into the DER/ASN.1 parsing routines without additional constraints beyond what production code might allow.
Network responses and certificate data consumed by KeyStoreHelper.decryptString and UbikeyJni.jdecrypt can be influenced by an attacker (malicious server, MitM, or crafted certificate files).
limitations: Ghidra MCP backend was unreachable, so no direct decompilation of libUbikeyJni.so functions was possible; assessment relies on symbol names and crash stack only.
Exact memcpy arguments (source, destination, length) and any partial bounds checks in the code could not be examined, leaving some uncertainty about exploitability.

chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP backend is unavailable (connection refused), so I cannot decompile or inspect the native functions; I must base the assessment purely on the provided metadata.

The crash is in __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> DecryptCertExchange -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt; this is clearly an application-level crypto/ASN.1 routine within libUbikeyJni.so, not just a harness artifact.

A memcpy crash during DER/ASN.1 decoding strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write bug (e.g., using a length from the encoded data without validating against the buffer size, or misparsed length fields). This is a common class of vulnerabilities in custom ASN.1/DER parsers.

The JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt is called from Java KeyStoreHelper.decryptString(String). The Java callgraph shows several paths where decryptString() is used with values that originate from network responses (Retrofit callbacks) and from app protocol/session handlers.

Because decryptString(String) is reachable from onResponse() handlers for retrofit2/okhttp calls, the decrypted data can be influenced by remote servers or possibly intermediaries (e.g., man-in-the-middle on an unpinned TLS channel, compromised backend, or other network input modelling in the fuzzer). Therefore, attacker-controlled or at least untrusted ciphertext can reach the native jdecrypt routine.

Inside jdecrypt, the ciphertext/string is almost certainly converted to bytes and fed into DecryptCertExchange / Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. If Decode_DER_ASN1_ST mishandles length fields and passes a wrong length to memcpy, a malformed ciphertext or ASN.1 blob can trigger the memcpy crash.

Because the crash happens at __memcpy rather than a hard-coded abort, it is indicative of memory corruption (OOB access). memcpy in system libc will segfault or abort when given an invalid pointer/length combination as a result of unsafe app code, which qualifies as a memory safety bug.

Even though I cannot inspect the exact memcpy call arguments, the function names (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST) and stack signature match a classic pattern: ASN.1 decoder walks DER data and copies fields based on lengths encoded inside the blob. If length parsing is flawed, this leads to out-of-bounds operations. This is a high-likelihood vulnerability pattern (CWE-787).

There is no evidence that the harness did something unrealistic like passing null pointers or impossible JNI arguments; instead, the harness simply drives fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt, mimicking normal usage. The same JNI function is used in production flows, so the path is realistic and reachable.

Because the data source is network/protocol traffic and the bug is a memcpy-based crash in a parser/decoder, the issue likely allows at least denial of service and potentially more serious memory corruption, depending on allocator layout and whether writes go out-of-bounds.

Lack of low-level decompilation slightly reduces confidence, but the combination of stack trace, context (ASN.1 decode), and Java callgraph is strong enough to classify as a likely real vulnerability with high severity.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy during Decode_DER_ASN1_ST / DecryptCertExchange, consistent with an out-of-bounds copy in ASN.1/DER parsing.

JNI entry jdecrypt is called by KeyStoreHelper.decryptString(), which is in turn invoked on data from retrofit2/okhttp network responses and session protocol handlers, so attacker-influenced input can reach the vulnerable code.
The failing code resides in libUbikeyJni.so and is part of production decryption/ASN.1 routines, not a test-only harness, so the path is realistically reachable in the app.

evidence.note: Top of native stack; crash implies invalid src/dst/len arguments from app-level code.
Function name and position above __memcpy indicate custom ASN.1/DER decoder doing buffer copies.
JNI entry used by KeyStoreHelper.decryptString(), tying native crash to Java-visible API reachable from network-driven flows.
Java-side decrypt helper used in Retrofit onResponse handlers; its input is derived from backend/network data.

recommendations: Manually reverse and audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and DecryptCertExchange for all memcpy/memmove calls; ensure lengths are validated against actual buffer sizes and DER/ASN.1 length fields are fully bounds-checked.
Add robust input validation in the JNI jdecrypt path (e.g., check ciphertext/ASN.1 blob length before passing to native decoder) and handle parse failures gracefully instead of proceeding to memcpy.
Instrument with ASan/UBSan in a test build and extend fuzzing on the jdecrypt interface using structured ASN.1/DER corpora to confirm and localize the exact out-of-bounds condition.
assumptions: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST implements custom DER/ASN.1 decoding and uses memcpy directly on attacker-influenced or semi-trusted buffers.
Network-originated data processed in Retrofit onResponse flows can reach KeyStoreHelper.decryptString without complete sanitization.
The fuzz harness approximates realistic usage of jdecrypt and does not introduce impossible pointer/ownership patterns.
limitations: Unable to decompile or inspect libUbikeyJni.so functions due to MCP backend connectivity failure; memcpy argument provenance is inferred from stack trace only.
Exact form of the memory corruption (read vs write, offset, controllability) is unknown, so exploitability beyond DoS is not fully established.
Java source for KeyStoreHelper.decryptString and surrounding flows was not directly inspected; taint from network inputs is inferred from method names and callgraph.
